data.title: Zeta Global: Bullish Catalysts Brewing
data.description:
Zeta Global (ZETA) keeps accumulating positive catalysts, which makes long-term investors like me happy to own shares of such a great company. The Marigold acquisition has been successfully completed in late November, which was the reason why the management increased FY2025 and FY2026 guidance. Therefore, we can conclude that the business combination goes on smoothly and all the expected synergies will be eventually unlocked. Recent news about platform usage was also very inspiring. Financial performance remains strong, and ZETA is still quite attractively valued even after a 4.3% share price increase since my previous call. I am adding more shares to my existing ZETA holding and confirm a Strong buy rating for the stock.

Reasons to continue buying ZETA

Zeta's financial performance remains strong, which is evident from the company's Q3 earnings release. It was a double beat versus consensus, which is always good in improving the company's image of the one that is committed to deliver on its promises. Revenue growth was solid once again with a 26% YoY increase. Operating leverage is strong because Zeta's EPS growth significantly outpaced revenue growth with a 69% growth on a YoY basis, from $0.16 to $0.27. According to Seeking Alpha, ZETA's outstanding shares count grew by just 1.5% YoY, meaning that the effect of dilution was limited on the quarterly EPS figure. Therefore, we can conclude that Zeta continues delivering high-quality EPS growth, i.e. powered by operating leverage and not some one-off events. Q3 2025 was also marked with a strong free cash flow ("FCF") growth, from $37.1 million to $57.6 million. It implies a 55% YoY growth in FCF, which underscores the high quality of Zeta's operating leverage as the EPS to FCF conversion remains quite high. To wrap up Zeta's strong Q3 earnings release, the company's financial position has improved as the net cash position increased by around $21 million sequentially.

I started my analysis with the latest earnings review because it helps the company build a rock-solid foundation for future growth. Improving net cash position together with increasing liquidity positions Zeta to reinvest in new growth projects. More investments in growth make me more optimistic as the business model has already proved itself efficient, which we see as the bottom line is growing faster than the top line. The company's R&D expenses grew by almost 67% if we compare Q3 2025 to Q3 2023, which is aggressive growth. Besides expanding organic growth potential due to increased investments in R&D, Zeta also recently proved itself as a successful business acquirer.

A few weeks ago, the company announced that it is raising financial guidance for Q4 2025 and for FY2026 after successful completion of the Marigold acquisition. Just for a brief recap, Marigold was acquired for aggregate consideration of up to $325 million to expand Zeta's global footprint and enhance AI-capabilities of Zeta Marketing Platform. I am impressed by the promptness of the positive effect on Zeta's financial performance from this acquisition. In the below table I have summarized the effect from Marigold acquisition completion on Zeta's revenue and adjusted EBITDA. The best part is that Marigold is already profitable in terms of EBITDA despite its relatively small scale. Moreover, it is also a business with solid operating leverage potential as its FY2026 adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to increase by more than two percentage points compared to Q4 2025. Clearly demonstrated early wins from Marigold's acquisition looks promising from the long-term shareholder value creation viewpoint.

Finally, recent data from the holiday shopping weekend between Thanksgiving and Cyber Monday showed that enterprises rely on Zeta's platform more aggressively. According to the release, overall usage surged by 153% YoY while Zeta's AI agent activity increased by staggering 25 times. This dynamic shows that adoption of Zeta's new offerings like agentic AI is accelerating, which underscores the company's ability to create value for customers. With successful M&A endeavors and new products developed organically, Zeta is firmly positioned to capitalize on the thriving marketing technology industry.

Last but not least, Zeta is still very attractively valued. Strong continued revenue momentum together with operating leverage is a solid combination to believe in rapid forward P/E ratio contraction. According to Wall Street analysts, the estimated 2028 multiple is just 13.7. This is a very cheap valuation for a marketing technology company with AI exposure. And it is also important to note that I am no just blindly following what Wall Street says but also have an opportunity to cross-check from the FCF side. According to the management's "Zeta 2028" strategic plan, the 2028 FCF target is $340 million. Dividing Zeta's current market cap of $4.8 billion by this forecasted FCF implies a forward estimated FY2028 P/FCF ratio of around 14. Due to the management's commitment to deliver on their targets and historically high EPS to FCF conversion, I think that the long-term forward P/FCF estimate that relies on "Zeta 2028" is a reliable one.

Reasons to avoid ZETA

Zeta accumulates vast amounts of sensitive data, which means that there is a substantial cybersecurity risk. In case of a data breach, the company's operations might be disrupted. Moreover, being a company that is unable to protect data is always bad for this company's reputation. Besides reputational risks associated with a potential data breach, there might be costly legal consequences.

The information might be particularly interesting for cybercriminals as there are dozens of large companies among Zeta's customers. Besides the cybersecurity risk, the fact that ZETA serves more than 40% of Fortune 100 companies means revenue concentration risks. I think so because companies from the Fortune 100 list are all large ones. With that being said, significant dependence on several major customers is risky because it makes Zeta's financial performance vulnerable in case one of its largest clients experiences setbacks.

In such a rapidly growing industry like digital marketing, competition risks are inevitably high. Moreover, Zeta's relatively low capital expenses every year far below $10 million per year means high probability of new market entrants since no large upfront investments are needed. Besides potential new entrants, there are digital advertising giants like Google, Meta, and Amazon. These companies are constantly looking to unlock new growth opportunities and if they decide to expand into Zeta's field it might be very difficult to compete with these giants due to their vast resources.

The verdict

ZETA is very attractively valued for a rapidly growing marketing technology business with AI exposure, while fundamentals are clearly improving. The company is well-positioned to capitalize on a favorable secular MarTech trend, meaning that ZETA remains a Strong Buy.

data.title: Cross Country Healthcare: The Failed Merger Is Disappointing, But Selling Here Would Be A Mistake
data.description:
On Wednesday, December 3rd, 2025, the stock of Cross Country Healthcare (CCRN) fell 6.16% after the company didn't release an update to their merger agreement with Aya Healthcare. December 3rd was a fallback deadline specified in the merger agreement, and CCRN was negotiating with Aya to extend it.

Then, on the morning of December 4th, CCRN announced the deal had been terminated. So we've reached the endgame: the Aya-CCRN merger has fallen through, and the stock is down 19% to $7.66 at the time of writing. It was Aya who terminated the merger, and reading between the lines, my guess is Aya did not want to pay $18.61 for a stock that was trading at roughly half that price, especially in the face of potentially more regulatory pushback.

It's a disappointing result for investors like myself who were betting on the deal completing. But it shouldn't come as a complete surprise, either. In this article, I turn our attention to CCRN's value as a standalone company as well as post-mortem the merger. My main takeaway is that two things came out of left field that may have impacted the merger: the death of Aya CEO Alan Braynin, and the recent government shutdown. I also think that CCRN is a Buy purely on fundamentals, and has multiple short-term catalysts that could cause a bounce from the 52-week lows the stock currently sits at.

Q3 2025 Earnings

Since my last article, CCRN posted their third quarter earnings report. Top line revenue came in at $250 million, down 20% YoY from $315 million and down 8.8% from last quarter's $274 million. The losses are basically exclusively in the Nurse and Allied Staffing segment, with Physician Staffing basically flat. Those are the only two revenue segments. Nurse and Allied Staffing is $668 million for the nine months of 2025, while Physician Staffing is $149 million. For 2024, those figures were $888m and $145m respectively.

The balance sheet remains strong, with $295 million in current assets compared to $130 million in total liabilities, for $165 million in tangible book value. That's roughly $5 of tangible value on a per share basis. The market cap of CCRN is $267 million at the time of writing, or $8.16 per share. The book value is $408 million, down from $419 million at the start of 2025, for a price/book ratio of 0.65. And this is without the incoming $20 million termination fee from Aya, which is about $0.60 on a per share basis.

Cash flow is also positive for the year, with $24 million in free cash flow over the first nine months of 2025. For the same time period last year, this figure was $90 million.

Using Seeking Alpha's comparison tool, I put CCRN side by side with fellow healthcare staffing stock AMN, and they both tell a similar story of dirt cheap valuations following a crash in revenues and profits. CCRN has $3 in cash per share and a 0.23 price/sales ratio. AMN has the same price/sales ratio, but unlike CCRN, AMN has a substantial amount of debt ($884 million).

CCRN next reports earnings on or around March 4th, 2026, according to Zacks.

Share Buybacks and Insider Buying

CEO John Martins gave a typical upbeat message on the 8-K announcing the termination of the merger, but what stood out was his intention to buy back CCRN shares:

Under our current repurchase program, the Company also has authority to repurchase up to $40 million of its stock from time-to-time in the open market or in privately negotiated transactions. The Company intends to immediately commence repurchases, subject to market conditions.

At the time of writing, $40 million in share repurchases would make up just under 15% of the market cap of CCRN and I think buying back shares at this depressed price is a great move by management. Insider buying has also kicked off, with director Larry Cash buying $40,000 worth of stock at $8.17 per share. I would not be surprised to see other directors follow suit.

Insiders own 5.4% of the outstanding shares of CCRN according to the latest proxy statement, with cofounder and chairman of the board Kevin Clark accounting for 2% on his own.

Takeaways From the Merger

The way I see it, there were two black swan events with the Aya-CCRN merger. The first was the passing of Aya CEO Alan Braynin in May 2025. Braynin was one half of the two CEOs who made the deal, and I outlined in my last article how it seemed that CCRN's CEO John Martins and Braynin had a close relationship. Perhaps Aya would've been less likely to back out of the deal if he were still leading the company.

But I think Braynin's death wasn't as detrimental to the merger as the second unexpected event: the longest government shutdown in US history. For a recap, I'll refer to management's update from the Q3 earnings:

As of August 29, 2025, each of [CCRN] and Aya Healthcare had certified to the FTC that it had substantially complied with the Second Request and, as a result of discussions with the FTC, the HSR waiting period was set to expire on November 17, 2025. In addition, the Aya Merger end date was extended from September 3, 2025 to December 3, 2025. However, the FTC is closed during the government shutdown and the expiry of the HSR waiting period extends day-for-day while the government shutdown continues. As a result, absent any further agreement from the FTC, the HSR waiting period is now set to expire after the end date contemplated by the Merger Agreement, which is currently December 3, 2025.

The government shutdown was 44 days long, so the expiration date for the HSR waiting period would've been pushed back to December 30. If the December 3rd deadline hadn't been passed, the FTC would've had to either push back harder on the deal, or let the waiting period expire, and the companies would've been able to merge. The government shutdown let Aya get past the December 3rd deadline and back away from the deal.

So I see these two events, the death of a CEO and a government shutdown, as things that came out of left field and hurt the deal's chances. But certainly there were more predictable signs that the deal had slim odds. A press release by the FTC confirmed what we already knew from the Second Request: that the FTC had found "significant competitive concerns" with the merger.

Conclusion

A big motto in probabilistic fields like sports or poker is "process over outcome", meaning that sometimes a good process yields bad results, or vice versa, due to luck. My "process" for CCRN was that it was a good deterministic bet on the merger going through given the price of the stock, and I roughly stand by this. We knew that mergers had lower odds of completing after getting a second request from the FTC, with about 25% of mergers finishing at that point. I thought that the merger had more than a 25% chance of going through, and that, given the intrinsic value of the underlying business as well as the potential upside, the stock had positive expected value for investors.

What I didn't anticipate was how low the stock price would go if the deal fell through. I explicitly guided towards the $9-11 range, and we ended up with lows in the $7.50 area and currently sit just above $8. I think this is overdone, and I am keeping a buy rating for CCRN.

With the $20 million Aya fee, as well as the planned share buybacks and potential for more insider buying, I see multiple short-term catalysts that could get CCRN rerated higher. In the long term, if travel nursing bounces back in any way, the business could see potential upside. Although the story with CCRN has shifted from a merger arbitrage to a deep value stock, I'll be holding my existing shares and buying more at these depressed levels.

data.title: ASML: Growth Outlook Strong, Upside Limited At This Multiple
data.description:
The Dutch chip-equipment manufacturing leader ASML (ASML) has rallied over 60% year-to-date, and now it is a more than $400 billion market-cap company. The market rewarded ASML due to consistent performance and solid top and bottom-line growth.

The semiconductor company now trades at 39x earnings multiple versus FY2025 EPS estimate, and roughly 37x versus FY2026 EPS forecast. This is not cheap. Compared to the FY2026 EPS estimate, it represents approximately a 54% premium versus peers at 24x, and nearly 61% versus the average company in the S&P500 at 23x earnings multiple. This seems to be too pricey to chase, and caps the upside in the short term.

I think that ASML is an outstanding, high-quality business, but the market already knows it. The Dutch leader has a low-leveraged capital structure, delivers steady top and bottom, while maintaining above-market margins, but valuation already reflects that.

That is why I rate ASML a Hold. I think it may appreciate in the stock price, followed by bottom-line expansion, but I also wouldn't be surprised if the stock slightly consolidates from these premium levels. I forecast a $1,176 price target over the next year, implying a 6% upside possibility, but unlikely to outperform the benchmark. It remains a solid business, but given its elevated multiple, it seems that the market already knows it.

Q3 2025: Miss On The Top-Line, But Beat On The Bottom-Line

On October 15, the company delivered Q3 2025 results. This seemed to be a mixed quarterly performance, as although ASML posted a beat on the bottom-line result, the revenue result came in below analysts' estimates. Nevertheless, the stock was up 3% on the earnings day.

ASML delivered $8.75 billion in revenue, highlighting a 7.60% growth over the past 12 months, but $224 million below what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $6.39 in diluted EPS, an 11% on a year-over-year basis versus $5.75 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, and $0.17 above analysts' estimates. I think the single-digit growth in revenue appears to be slightly concerning, and that contributes to my neutral stance. If top-line decelerates, and the trend continues in the future, the upside appears capped.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted that ASML generated EUR5.6 billion in net system sales, accounting for 75% of the total revenue. The installed base management sales reached EUR2 billion. The top-line performance argues that ASML remains a well-diversified business across segments. The system sales are leading revenue performance, and it captured a split of 52% for ArF immersion revenue, while EUV lithography accounted for 38% of the total sales.

I would also like to emphasize that AMSL heavily benefited from favorable FX. The slide below highlights that on a EUR basis, the top-line performance has increased by half a percent. If no favorable FX tailwind, the company's top-line has stagnated, and it argues for my consolidation narrative.

In terms of end-use, it accounts for 35% memory and 65% logic, making it less dependent on sole end-use. China has meaningfully contributed to the top-line performance, as shipments have surged from 27% of total deliveries to 42% on a quarter-over-quarter basis. Japan, EMEA, the US, and Taiwan contracted in terms of the percentage of total contribution. Investors should monitor, as high dependency on China could lead to a discounted earnings multiple because of elevated concentration risk.

It has also indicated that ASML achieved 51.6% in gross margin, while operating margin arrived at 32.8%. The net income accounted for 28.3% of total sales. I think these profitability metrics imply an above-market performance, arguing for an elevated valuation. For instance, in terms of the net income margin (TTM) of approximately 29%, ASML operates at roughly 5.8x outperformance versus peers at 5%. If the Dutch leader can sustain premiums, I wouldn't be surprised if ASML traded at a substantial premium further.

Furthermore, the semiconductor company is also exposed to meaningful tailwinds related to broader market growth. For instance, the global lithography market was valued at $28 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to reach $55 billion by 2032. This represents a 9% CAGR, and if the company can sustain a leading position in the space, it appears to be well-positioned to benefit from the broader market expansion.

In Q3 2025, ASML has also indicated that it continues a shareholder-friendly capital approach. Besides the quarterly dividend, it has repurchased roughly 218,000 shares, resulting in about EUR148 million. Furthermore, the company anticipates the announcement of a new share buyback program in January 2026. If it includes a significant amount authorized for a share repurchase program, this may heavily support bottom-line growth over the following years.

On the upside, ASML presents a business and margin stability due to well-managed costs. Both R&D and SG&A costs remained roughly flat over the past year. However, due to top-line stagnation in EUR, investors should monitor whether costs don't pick up from here. If that happens, margins may contract, and investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the ASML stock.

To conclude, this was a mixed performance, and due to a favorable FX rate, the company's top and bottom-line growth appeared greater than it actually was. However, management guides for a strong finish to FY2025. It remains well-positioned to deliver double-digit top-line growth, but if we exclude FX, I think the result would signal high-single-digit performance. It will be interesting to see how it plays out, but at the current price tag, it seems to be difficult to justify premium expansion; thus, supporting my Hold stance.

ASML Appears Expensive, And Too Pricey To Chase

ASML currently trades at a forward P/E of about 39. Although the chart below highlights this is a business-typical valuation, given its recent performance, I argue the stock is expensive and may be due for consolidation.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 2.19, ASML appears to be trading at 26% premium versus the sector median of 1.74. It remains insignificant, and may argue for uptrend continuation in the stock price, if top and bottom-line growth meaningfully resume.

On a forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 11.28 and 11.36, respectively, ASML appears to be trading at 3x overvaluation at the midpoint versus peers at 3.49 and 3.55. Valuation metrics imply that the market anticipates a meaningful top-line growth, and I don't think that high-single-digit performance, if we exclude FX, justifies a premium multiple. In addition to this, ASML remains heavily dependent on AI-driven tailwinds, and if sentiment dramatically shifts, top-line could correct, too, alongside earnings multiple.

The Dutch semiconductor manufacturing company has a low-leveraged capital structure, arguing for a bullish continuation. Although the company has $3.17 billion in total obligations, it also has $6 billion in cash. The capital structure reveals ASML could pay its debt with ease, and remains well-positioned to operate in both high and low interest rate environments. To this, following the company's reference to the expected share repurchase program in FY2026, this supports a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach, indicating strong liquidity and low leverage.

Over the past 12 months, ASML delivered a 23% revenue growth, but it's important to emphasize a few things. To begin with, the company's performance remains volatile, with one quarter posting an 11% top-line decline, which is then followed by 16% growth on a year-over-year basis. Moreover, the recent performance highlighted a slowdown in revenue growth, and investors should monitor whether this is a more structural issue. Also, long-term investors should consider FX headwind if the EURUSD pair experiences a shift in the trend.

ASML operates at premium margins, and that partially supports a premium multiple. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 53% indicates an 8% increase versus the sector median of 49%. The EBIDTA margin (TTM) of 38% highlights a 3.5x outperformance versus peers at 11%. If the semiconductor leading can sustain such margins further, this would support a bullish outlook.

The Dutch leader remains a strong company that has a solid market position and experiences momentum in demand. However, it trades at a substantial premium, which argues that the market may have already priced in the company's future growth. If results arrive weaker-than-anticipated, the downside is not protected. I remain cautious, especially due to recent performance, which would indicate a flat yearly performance, if not the FX tailwind.

Wall Street anticipates $30.15 in diluted EPS for FY2026, and this represents a 5% growth on a year-over-year basis. I agree with the estimate, as I think the company may be due for consolidation. I also think that ASML may mirror bottom-line performance; thus, if we apply approximately the current earnings multiple of 39x to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $30.15, we arrive at my price target of $1,176. It will be interesting to monitor whether the forecast plays out.

Main Risks And Concerns

ASML is tied to risks and concerns that could weigh on the stock price performance in the short term.

The company is heavily dependent on AI-powered demand. If sentiment dramatically shifts, or IT spending slows down, or the heavy Capex investments do not translate into meaningful top and bottom line growth, the AI-related names may correct. If that happens, ASML is likely to follow the trend, too.

Based on the recent quarterly performance, if we exclude a favorable FX rate, it seems that the company's top-line result was flat on a year-over-year basis. Although this could be a temporary hiccup, which is expected to pick up in the following quarter, it may also highlight a more structural issue. If the trend persists, this could significantly weigh on the company's performance and result in a correction.

The recent quarterly performance has also indicated revenue concentration risk in China. It has accounted for 42% of total shipments in the region, and if geopolitical friction emerges between the EU and China, the company's future may be clouded. In addition to this, there has been a shift in geographic trend on a quarter-over-quarter basis, with declining performance in the US, Japan, and the rest of Asia. If this isn't also a temporary hiccup, I think investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the company's stock.

On the other hand, if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, I think ASML remains a solid player in the space, whose stock price may appreciate in accordance with its bottom-line growth.

ASML Is A Hold

Although I think ASML is a high-quality company that remains well-positioned to drive the AI-powered wave, its valuation appears to be too expensive to chase. If we exclude the FX tailwind, it may seem that it experiences a slowdown in top-line performance, and if this is a feature of a more structural issue, it may correct from premium levels.

Furthermore, based on the recent quarterly performance, it seems that the company has a revenue concentration risk and a high dependency on demand in China. If the AI-powered demand declines or sentiment around AI shifts, the company's performance may erode, and investors may not be willing to pay a premium for the company's stock. This presents a mixed picture, and I don't think it is well-positioned to outperform the benchmark in the short term.

Thus, I rate ASML a Hold with a $1,176 price target over the next year. This presents a 6% upside possibility. 38 Wall Street analysts imply a $1,062 price target over the next year, signaling a 4% downside potential. This is definitely a possibility, and it will be interesting to see how it will play out for the Dutch company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sleep Number Navigates A Challenging Consumer Market When Time Is Critical
data.description:
Sleep Number Corporation (SNBR) is wading through a challenging consumer market as new management aims to aggressively cut costs while sparking growth in the coming fiscal year. With the goal of targeting a broader range of cost-conscious consumers, the firm is revamping its product offerings while weighing a new go-to-market strategy. With its credit agreement recently amended to provide more financial flexibility in the coming quarters, SNBR is on the clock to return to profitability in order to maintain good standing. Given the weighted risks, I am recommending SNBR shares with a Sell rating with a price target of $5.64/share at 0.10x FY26 price/sales.

Though SNBR shares may be viewed as a deep value strategy, investors should be cautious as this very well may be a value trap. If electing to invest in the turnaround strategy, I recommend strict risk management procedures with a trailing stop-loss in order to protect your investment.

Sleep Number Corporation Operations

SNBR has been undergoing a major transformation in recent years as the firm has been faced with a steep decline in revenue, offloading fixed assets and leases in order to rightsize the balance sheet. SNBR announced a company-wide restructuring in Q4’23 to reduce operating expenses and improve margins. Cost-cutting measures include the termination of its strategic-development partner contract as well as lease termination and a workforce reduction. In total, SNBR has spent $81.34mm in restructuring charges since first announcing the restructuring in Q4’23. In turn, SNBR has reduced its operating costs by $115mm year-to-date, aiming to achieve $130mm by the end of FY25.

In addition to the organization, SNBR reshuffled its C-suite, bringing in a new President, CEO & Director, Linda Findley, effective April 7, 2025, and Amy O’Keefe as CFO on December 8, 2025. SNBR also appointed Phillip M. Eyler as Independent Chair effective at the conclusion of the 2025 Annual Meeting.

A core component of the restructuring is to bring to market new product offerings with the intent to reach a new customer base while simplifying its operating model. As part of this, management is expecting to offer more affordable products with features consumers are seeking out, likely offering lower-end products that provide more value.

One of the challenges SNBR may be faced with as it exits FY25 and enters FY26 is the challenging consumer discretionary environment. McKinsey & Company reported its Q4’25 consumer trends in early December 2025, suggesting that 75% of consumers are trading down in at least one category in terms of their budgets, largely driven by concerns relating to the cost of living and job security. Though SNBR primarily caters to the higher-end consumer, the firm’s refocus on the broader market might be met with some hesitancy in the near-term if consumers continue to tighten their discretionary budgets. Specific to SNBR, consumers have lowered their expectations for spending on home furnishings and furniture for Q4’25, potentially impacting SNBR’s ability to achieve its sales target for the quarter.

The employment environment may also be facing challenges going into the end of 2025. The ADP National Employment Report for November 2025 indicated a -32,000 decline in employment, largely driven by a pullback in small companies with stronger hiring across mid- and large employers. From an industry perspective, job declines were largely tied to blue collar workers in information, financial services, and professional services.

Sleep Number Corporation Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q4’25, I’m forecasting SNBR to report $325mm in net sales with a diluted EPS of -$1.48/share. Given the challenging consumer market and potential cost-cutting headwinds, I believe SNBR may face another challenging quarter with the expectation of a further decline in sales paired with improving operating margins. Given management’s aggressive cost-cutting measures, I’m forecasting SNBR to return to profitability in FY27 with FY26 being a trough year.

SNBR closed Q3’25 with $1.26mm in cash & equivalent and $579.5mm in borrowings for a leverage ratio of 6.80x net debt/aEBITDA, a steep increase from previous years driven by increased borrowings under its revolver and declining aEBITDA.

In Q3’25, management indicated that SNBR has secured an amendment to extend its bank agreement through 2027, alleviating certain risks to pay down its revolver. As part of its amended credit facility, SNBR reduced its borrowing capacity by $10mm to $475mm and will further reduce its capacity to $465mm on July 31, 2026. SNBR also gained some breathing room for its outstanding leverage covenant, increasing the cap to 5.25x through the end of FY25 and reducing to 4.75x for Q1’26, 4.80x for Q2’26, and 4x going forward. As of Q3’25, SNBR had $82mm available under its existing credit facility. As of Q3’25, SNBR had a debt-service coverage ratio [DSCR] of 1.04x, calculated as adjusted EBITDA over interest expense.

Considering SNBR’s financial position, challenging consumer market, and restructuring, SNBR may be in a challenging position in terms of bolstering operations in order to widen its debt coverage, potentially impacting the company’s ability to make interest payments on its outstanding borrowings.

As part of the restructuring program, SNBR may be rolling off more fixed assets while consolidating its real estate footprint. This may mean that the firm will have a relatively lower capital reinvestment rate with respect to depreciation in the coming quarters as the firm rightsizes its operational footprint.

A major challenge SNBR may be facing is the significant reduction in media spend, narrowing its marketing budget to cut down on costs. Though management is aiming to transition more sales to its digital platform, a reduction in marketing may create challenges at the top-line, and may hinder the firm’s ability to further reach its target audience for mattress sales. Nonetheless, the firm is aiming to lean more heavily into its sales channels more effectively, which may involve offloading marketing costs to channel partners. Theoretically, the restructuring of its revolver may provide certain flexibility to improving its marketing spend to draw in new business.

Risks Related to Sleep Number Corporation

Bull Case

SNBR is currently undergoing a major restructuring in an effort to cut costs while managing its elevated debt position. Given the significant share price pullback over the last five years, shareholders could potentially be rewarded if SNBR is successful in turning back to profitability while working down its bloated debt position.

Bear Case

SNBR faces mounting debt and interest payments that may result in increased risk of not being covered by operations. With the consumer environment weakening, SNBR may be navigating a challenging consumer market in the coming quarters that may add pressure to maintaining its debt covenants.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

SNBR shares are currently trading at 0.12x price/sales, a significant discount by historical levels. Despite the deep discount, SNBR faces certain financial risks that may further drive the share price down. SNBR’s market capitalization makes up a mere 24% of its total enterprise value, suggesting that investors may be concerned with the firm’s ability to successfully restructure and work its way out of mounting debt.

Given the challenging environment for the end of 2025, I am recommending SNBR shares with a Sell rating with a price target of $5.64/share at 0.10x price/sales.

In the instance of a successful turnaround, shares could have a potential intrinsic value of $39/share; however, SNBR shares very well could be a value trap. From a weighted risk perspective, SNBR shares may not be an appropriate investment for most investors as the turnaround may take multiple years before realizing any value.

Multiples Model Description

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

DCF Model Description

The DCF model can be read with the array of discount rates at the top and terminal growth rates to the left of the model. The forecasted cash flows are denoted at the bottom of the model. The share price array ties the respective discount rates and terminal growth rates. The highlighted prices denoted in the model associate with my expected range for intrinsic value per share.

data.title: Albertsons: Failed Kroger Deal Fallout Creates An Attractive Entry Point
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Albertsons Companies (ACI) is currently trading at some of their lowest levels since going public, marked by a failed merger, lawsuits and macro uncertainty, but offering what seems to be a compelling opportunity.

Right now, the company is going through a significant cost-reduction program and aiming at improving its operations while also focusing on digital and private-label brands, standing to benefit from macro tailwinds while also doing huge buybacks, with a massive, accelerated buyback program announced recently.

Albertsons reported a solid Q2 back in October, beating expectations and delivering $331.5 million in free cash flow during H1'FY25, although that's below the $421.8 million recorded during the same period a year ago, mostly explained by changes in working capital. This comes as the company held a stable cash flow over the past years, with a solid $749.4 million in free cash flow reported in FY24, $628.2 million in FY23 and $700 million in FY22.

As for the guidance, Albertsons increased the lower end of their sales growth range to 2.2% to 2.75% and raised their adjusted net income per common share range to $2.06 to $2.19, while also increasing the lower end of their CAPEX and expecting $1.85 billion at midpoint, marking a decrease compared to the $1.93 billion last fiscal year or over $2 billion in the years before that.

This increase in CAPEX comes as they accelerate their investments in digital and automation, which I'll mention below.

Financially, based on ACI's latest report, we see a relatively normal position for a company in this industry, with the current assets below their current liabilities but quite a bit of assets to more than cover their debt.

As we can see, the recent $700 million senior note offering used to repay their 3.25% notes due 2026 came at 5.5% and 5.75% yields, which is a direct effect of the higher-rate environment we're going through, so they definitely stand to benefit from continued rate cuts in the future.

Dividends & Buybacks

Albertsons currently pays a decent ~3.5% dividend yield, costing about $330 million per year now for a very sustainable payout ratio below 50%. Also, Albertsons announced an accelerated share buyback program with JPMorgan to repurchase $750 million worth of stock (and increased the program by the same amount), leaving about $1.3 billion for future buybacks. In total, we're talking about a massive yield, while the $1.3 billion in buybacks represent a ~13.75% total yield.

Risks & Opportunities

Of course, one of the key risks to keep in mind in this industry will always be the competition. As mentioned before, this industry is subject to very thin margins by default since what they do is not really groundbreaking and has been going on and improved over hundreds (or even thousands) of years. This doesn't make it a bad industry to be in, but increases the risk, and we're looking at more and more competition from all around the globe, even in the US.

This industry is dominated by massive players like Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT) or even Amazon (AMZN) nowadays with Whole Foods, while foreign stores such as Aldi, Ahold Delhaize (ADRNY) with their Food Lion or Schwarz Group through Lidl (Europe's largest retailer, with ~18% market share) are also gaining significant share and expanding, changing the dynamics of the entire market.

In fact, we can see Aldi being the number one company back in 2024 when it comes to growth both in terms of store count and square feet added - and it's worth noting that Albertsons is also high in this top. Aldi continued this aggressive expansion, planning to become the second-largest grocer after Walmart, which is definitely massive for something that began a bit over a decade ago. These companies also came with significant shifts to the market, on top of threatening smaller peers such as Albertsons.

I say that because there are several studies showing Lidl's or Aldi's impact wherever they enter, basically forcing local peers to reduce prices and also causing what's known as the "Lidl effect" or "Aldi effect" nowadays - basically pioneering the private-label market that's gaining significant traction lately.

I believe it's clear that these companies will continue to focus on private labels, as Albertson's own brands could technically be considered billion-dollar brands, representing about a quarter of their revenue and targeting ~30%, with the CEO highlighting during the fiscal Q2 Earnings Call:

These enhancements are driving customer engagement and loyalty, while also contributing to margin accretion through improved mix and merchandising. As we elevate the visibility and appeal of our own brands, we believe we can drive outsized growth in this critical area of our business, reinforcing our competitive advantage and long-term profitability as we drive penetration from 25% to 30% over time.

This comes as private labels offer significant advantages over traditional brands managed by giant corporations, allowing the company to control its own operations, improve margins significantly and offering massive power of negotiation against the traditional giants in the industry. Now and especially in the future, with private label brands available and growing, the food giants will have to fight for even less shelf space.

Despite the high competition, we've seen Kroger (KR) trying to buy ACI, although their October 2022 attempt of acquiring Albertsons for $24.6 billion was denied, with the FTC arguing that this would affect the environment by reducing competition at the expense of consumers and workers. I mentioned more about the failed deal in the Kroger analysis posted recently, but to be fair, that can be said about most acquisitions ever and this deal didn't look like such a big hit to consumers, workers or the competitive environment given their relatively small size in a giant market that's not only dominated by hundred-billion dollar giants, but also sees growing competition from new contenders.

As mentioned, it's even worse that this was the only notable M&A in the sector in 2024 that was blocked, although it was also significantly bigger. Albertsons actually sued Kroger shortly after the deal failed to go through, arguing that KR did not take the necessary steps and failed to take "any and all actions" to secure the regulatory approval - claiming that it breached their end of the agreement by "refusing to divest the necessary assets, ignoring feedback from regulators, rejecting divestiture buyers and failing to cooperate with Albertsons."

This lawsuit advanced and we've seen Albertsons even saying that Kroger had what they called a "classic case of buyer's remorse" after the negative reaction from the market/investors, while KR had its own arguments and allegations of a "surreptitious scheme" between Albertsons and C&S Wholesale Grocers, as mentioned in the previous analysis.

These lawsuits are still ongoing and the $4 billion special dividend Albertsons already paid due to the merger definitely affected them, while both companies changed their CEOs recently.

Similar to its peers, Albertsons is aggressively trying to integrate AI and consumer analytics through its "Albertsons Media Collective" and new AI Shopping Assistant, while also fortifying its omnichannel strategy through partnerships with companies like Instacart (CART), Uber Eats (UBER) and DoorDash (DASH) in recent years, with digital sales growing at a CAGR of 24% over 3 years and their pharmacy sales up 19% YoY as of Q2.

To fund this digital expansion, Albertsons also launched a productivity initiative aiming at saving $1.5 billion over the next three years (FY25 to FY27), recently announcing the closure of 29 of their underperforming stores and the planning to open 9 new ones by the end of 2025 as their portfolio optimization is advancing. These savings include a focus on supply chain automation, corporate restructuring, and further underperforming store closures.

Regarding tariffs, ACI's CFO also pointed out the following during the fiscal Q2 Call, highlighting the overall limited impact, although we should keep in mind that it can still hurt them indirectly by affecting their consumers and partners:

(...) as it relates to tariffs, tariffs have not had a material impact on our financial performance yet this year as 90% of the products we sell are sourced domestically, insulating us from global trade volatility. Beyond that, we have and are taking proactive steps to mitigate cost exposure, leveraging sourcing and supplier partnerships to minimize the downstream impact to both our margins and our customers. And with that, I'll hand it back to Susan for closing remarks.

Rate cuts should also be a key opportunity, as the company's clients have been struggling with lower discretionary budgets and high inflation for years, while the company also faced higher costs of debt as a result of the hikes, limiting their ability to expand. In the future, this can result in cheaper debt combined with improving demand as economic conditions improve, so it's no wonder why the market pays close attention to the Fed's moves.

However, the political environment is also something to keep in mind, with potential risks coming from the SNAP benefits changes, with the recent changes passed through Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" Act taking effect from January 2026. This would increase the work requirement age limit, while people won't be able to use SNAP benefits for soda, candy, and items with high added sugar in some states, among others.

And that's on top of Trump's anti-immigration policies and the expectable anti-benefit politics, potentially hurting the demand and workforce market and potentially hurting the US as a whole, as rising workforce costs and lower demand would ultimately be paid by the consumer, especially when paired with tariffs.

So, ACI will have to navigate a very interesting environment, with plenty of risks and opportunities to be aware of. Although they have the tools and cash flows to adapt and they are on the right track with private labels and digital expansion, the CEO change combined with lawsuit and political uncertainty and competitive pressure also bring execution risks.

Another risk comes from the fact that Cerberus Capital Management owns about 27.6% of Albertsons, which in itself comes with significant influence over the company's future and decisions. After all, the fund was the main architect and beneficiary behind Albertsons paying a massive $4 billion special dividend ages before the Kroger deal was anywhere near complete, basically making the company pay that huge amount of cash for no concrete reason and securing a ton of cash in advance instead of waiting for the deal.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Kroger, Koninklijke Ahold Delhaize, Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM), Walmart, Costco (COST) and Target, we actually see the lowest P/CF ratio and a relatively low PB compared to the others, as ACI stands out from most other points of view as the cheapest in the group. The failed KR acquisition plays a massive role in this, as we can clearly see the initial drop caused by the proposed acquisition, which had Albertsons pay a massive special dividend, which they did.

Valuation

To be more conservative, I'll start by assuming a free cash flow of $725 million in FY25, below ACI's level from a year ago despite the lower CAPEX even at the highest end of their expectations. Given their cost savings initiatives and investments combined with the potential from rate cuts and private label, I'll go for a 6.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (below their 7.07% 5Y forward FCF/share average despite the environment and everything going on), followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I'll go for a WACC of 7.5% (above my estimated ~6.6% based on a cost of equity of 8.7% and 5.2% cost of debt) for a better margin of safety, with $7.86 billion in net debt (not including the operating lease obligations) and 562.5 million shares outstanding. This gets us to an enterprise value of $18.68 billion, an equity value of $10.82 billion and an intrinsic value of $19.24 per share, without taking into account any effect from the buybacks.

Conclusions

Albertsons is a Buy thanks to their solid fundamentals, reliable cash flows and solid digital plus pharma expansion combined with a major cost savings program. Again, there are risks to keep in mind, but the overall risk-reward seems to be in their favor at current levels, with potential for significant improvements thanks to their initiatives, industry trends and significant buybacks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: 2 BDCs With Unsustainable Yields
data.description:
There is a general consensus that the BDC (BIZD) yields are slowly but surely marching towards the chopping block. The key driver that is pushing the BDCs in that direction is the Fed's policy. As we have already evidenced it on the ground from the several cuts that have been made since mid-2024, whenever rates go down, the BDC earnings tend to follow.

The issue here, which really forms such a pessimistic consensus, is the combination of the following factors (I'm listing my Top 3, but obviously there are more):

  1. Interest rates are headed downwards.
  2. The BDC sector average base dividend coverage is 100%.
  3. Many BDCs have exhausted their balance sheets (i.e., target level leverage achieved from which funding new growth would be too risky).

This is a clear recipe for experiencing dividend cuts across the board. Very hard to argue against that.

Having said that, it is clearly not that, say, in Q1 2025 all the BDCs that have their dividend coverage levels tight will be collectively announcing lower dividends. Far from it.

We have to understand that the average includes many outliers, both on the positive and negative ends.

For example, some BDCs carry such ammunition as a) equity investment exposures that, if monetized, would easily cover dividends for quite some time; b) overcapitalized balance sheets that could accommodate leverage-driven growth; and c) P/NAV premiums, which could facilitate accretive equity issuances that would also help drive growth and thus fight back the earnings pressure. So, this is about the positive outliers.

Similarly, there is the negative end of the spectrum, which is where we will very likely see first cracks and dividend cuts.

In the article below, I will elaborate on two such BDCs, which, in my view, stand first in line in front of the chopping block.

#1 - PNNT

PennantPark Investment Corporation (PNNT) is a relatively standard BDC, which focuses on first-lien lending. Yet, the financial parameters are nowhere close to being standard. Let me unpack this.

First, PNNT has been dealing with declining earnings generation since late 2024 - i.e., each quarter has brought negative surprises. For example, in the most recent report, PNNT delivered core net investment income per share of $0.15, primarily due to a lower base rate and some spread compression.

Second, if we put the $0.15 per share figure in the context of a quarterly base dividend of $0.24 per share, we will arrive at a notable problem. Namely, the base dividend coverage level is among the worst in the BDC world and currently sits at 62.5%. It means that just a bit more than half of what PNNT has to pay out in dividends gets covered by earnings. The rest comes from leverage or received capital from loan repayment, which does not get reinvested back in growth.

Third, the actual cash dividend coverage is even worse if we adjust for the PIK factor, which accounts for ~14% of the total net investment income.

Fourth, theoretically, one might argue that PNNT could tap into its balance sheet to fuel additional loan funding, which would then in turn lift up the core net investment income figure. The reality is that this argument simply does not hold true since the leverage stands at 1.6x, which is one of the highest levels in the sector.

Fifth, and this is a bit more technical, a notable chunk of PNNT's earnings stems from the underlying joint ventures, which are not consolidated in the books. The issue is that within these structures there lies an even higher leverage level than what we can see on PNNT's balance sheet. For example, the largest joint venture generates an NII yield of 17%, which is significantly above the yield that PNNT extracts from its consolidated assets. So, if this JV starts to experience non-accruals, the consequences might be huge given the magnified effects that are associated with relatively aggressive leverage. Granted, it might not happen now, but sooner or later there simply should be some non-accrual pressures, which is completely logical for any BDC/private credit vehicle.

In a nutshell, it is hard to imagine how PNNT could keep the existing dividend intact for much longer than one or two quarters.

#2 - OCSL

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) is also an externally managed, first-lien-focused BDC with very little equity focus. The underlying core exposures to the direct lending factors are similar to PNNT.

As opposed to PFTL, OCSL has suffered some pain already this year in terms of delivering a steep dividend cut and receiving equity injection (support) from its private equity owner, Oaktree Capital Management.

The issue that OCSL faced was inflating non-accruals, which, coupled with the general headwinds (i.e., lower base rates and tighter spreads), inflicted significant damage to the NII per share generation.

In order to halt the NAV-funded dividend distribution process (which is currently the case with PNNT), the management decided to cut the dividend, inject fresh equity into the system, and introduce a temporary fee waiver.

While all of this did indeed provide an immediate benefit to OCSL's financials, the situation after the support package continued to worsen. In other words, OCSL recorded new non-accruals and faced the same pressures as most BDCs, mainly from lower base rates. This is why, on a YTD basis, OCSL is down ~16% (on a price return level).

Currently, the picture seems somewhat positive if we look at the leverage. OCSL has a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.02x, which could help the BDC generate fresh NII per share flows that could offset the pressures from forthcoming interest rate cuts (including the lagged effects that come from the most recent one).

Having said that, I think that OCSL is very close to cutting its dividend again.

I see three clear reasons.

First, the base dividend coverage has declined to 102.5%. Yet, we have to consider here a) ~11% PIK income of the total NII, b) slight support coming from waived incentive fees (this will disappear in 2026), and c) further headwinds from lower base rates. Even if OCSL steps up its loan funding game, I doubt that it will be enough to achieve a fully cash-funded dividend

Second, in the most recent quarter, OCSL did a solid job on the non-accrual front. However, given the historical dynamics, it would be only prudent if we assigned a decent probability for OCSL experiencing new non-accruals. If that happens, the earnings will for sure fall below the dividend level.

Third, and this is strongly related to the previous point, OCSL has ~23% of its portfolio placed in software businesses, which, in my view, are more exposed to AI risk than other, non-cyclical industries, which we, for example, in the Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) case, can see constitute ~95% of its portfolio. Now, if we put this together with the fact that in Q4 2025, OCSL's median portfolio company median EBITDA dropped by ~7% and leverage increased by 0.01x, I think that you will agree with me that it would make perfect sense to factor in some headwinds from software-driven non-accruals going forward.

In a nutshell, the story for OCLS's dividend cut is just as straightforward as for PNNT. The difference, however, is that OCSL could potentially keep the existing distribution level as it has some gas in the tank (leverage) to fuel portfolio growth, which could at least partially offset the earnings pressures that lie ahead. Plus, it does not have a massively underfunded dividend, as in PNNT's case. So, my base case scenario is that we will see OCSL slashing its dividend in mid-2026, where the event could happen sooner if OCSL reports another 2%+ of new non-accruals.

data.title: Academy Sports & Outdoors: Upgrade To Buy On Better Fundamental Outlook
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Academy Sports & Outdoors (ASO) previously with a hold rating stance because I struggled to see a clear catalyst that would cause an upwards re-rating. My view has changed after the latest print. The core business is proving far more resilient than I expected, the customer mix is shifting in the right direction, and the new-store opening strategy is showing tangible proof it works.

3Q 2025 Earnings Review

Starting with revenue, ASO delivered net sales of $1.38 billion, implying a growth of 3% y/y. Comparable sales growth [CSG] came in at -0.9% y/y, which was slightly below consensus expectations (note: BBG FY26 is actually FY25), but context matters here. The poorer-than-expected performance was mainly due to the ammunition category (part of the Outdoor segment), which saw a tough comparison to last year’s. This category alone saw a 130 bps headwind. If we back that out, the core business actually saw CSG of 0.4%. The other categories did well. Sports & Recreation grew 6% y/y; Apparel grew 3% y/y; and Footwear and Outdoors grew 2% y/y. As for profitability, I was worried previously that heavier promotions would hurt margins, but the opposite happened. Gross margin expanded 170 bps to 35.7%, and that stronger margin profile flowed directly to earnings. Adj. net income grew 9.6% y/y to $77.3 million, and adj. EPS grew 16.3% y/y to $1.14.

A Structural Shift in the Consumer Base

ASO’s exposure to households earning <$50k has been a concern of mine since my last update, and this cohort of customers continues to see decline again, down mid-single digits y/y. But the one new datapoint that got me to be less negative about this exposure is that broader mix shift. Traffic from households earning >$100k grew high single digits y/y, and this group now makes up ~40% of total sales. Historically, sales were split almost evenly across low-, middle-, and high-income tiers. This mix shift effectively improves ASO’s overall consumer profile and inherently makes it more resilient because lower-income consumers are the most sensitive to inflation and tend to pull back quickly. Higher-income shoppers, on the other hand, behave differently. They have more disposable income and can afford more discretionary spending. That is not to say they will splurge whenever they can. The current macro dynamic still “forces” them to be more value conscious, and I think ASO is in a sweet spot here, where it is delivering better value relative to specialty retailers (management said this during the call). They even noted this trend has been consistent for four quarters, which tells me it’s an ongoing trend.

One interesting point I took away from the call was that once these high-income shoppers walk into ASO’s doors, they aren’t just buying national brands; they’re actually buying ASO’s private labels like Magellan and Freely. This changes the narrative entirely because it creates a stickier relationship (if the product is good, chances are customers will come back to ASO to purchase again since it is exclusive to ASO).

For this entire chain of events to work, the one thing that matters is the “hook,” which ASO leverages its partnerships with premium brands to lure in these high-income shoppers. For instance, the brand's partnership with Nike is one of the key drivers of traffic, which has continued to gain momentum. The combined Nike and Jordan business grew high single digits y/y in Q3. Looking ahead, given the success of the rollout of Jordan into 145 doors (which has been especially strong), management plans to broaden both apparel and footwear access in 2026. These new products should capture the attention of the targeted high-income shoppers, as they want to continue buying their beloved brands but at a better value price.

Management expects the same pattern to hold, in that once these shoppers come for the Jordan brands, they would also look for Magellan outerwear or Freely apparel. There is somewhat of a flywheel effect ongoing here. The strong sell-through with Nike shows ASO is a solid brand partner to have, which makes it easier to secure additional premium brands, which gives it more “hooks” to lure in shoppers and therefore increase purchase of high-margin private label goods. In fact, we’re already seeing early signs of this happening with ASO adding the Carhartt brand and also specialized assortments like World Cup soccer balls.

The New Store Growth Engine Is Finally Working

One point I made in September was that ASO may have found a viable and scalable model to restart new store openings. It appears that I am right. Stores opened between 2022 and 2024 are not only performing well, but performance is also actually accelerating. In Q3, these older stores saw CSG of high single digits, a sharp acceleration from the low single digits in Q1 2025 and the mid-single digits in Q2 2025. To give a sense of how impressive this is. Consider that, historically, once a store hits its 14th month (enters the comp base calculation), it starts to contribute positively to the blended CSG figure. But for these 2022 to 2024 stores, management noted they contributed ~50 bps to the total comp in Q3, which is more than the entire blended CSG.

The impact will be even more apparent as more of these stores roll into the comp base, and with ASO planning to open another 20 to 25 stores in fiscal 2026, blended CSG should continue to go up for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

It makes sense that the market re-rated ASO’s valuation up from the average of ~7.5x NTM PE to 8.7x as of the time of writing. The fundamentals have clearly improved. The elevated inventory situation did not turn into a margin headwind; the shift in consumer profile is very positive; and ASO's new unit growth strategy should continue to drive CSG increase.

Management guidance for FY26 implies adj. EPS to fall by low single digits at the midpoint, but I do expect earnings growth to accelerate, as Q3 showed us the potential (adj. EPS was up ~16%). The consensus estimate for ASO in FY28 is ~$8.2, implying a low-teens CAGR, which I think is fine looking at Q3 numbers and the fundamentals. Say that ASO trades at 8.7x FY28 adj. EPS; this implies a share price of $71.

Risk

The biggest near-term risk is price elasticity. If the macro situation gets a lot worse from here, say it dips into a recession, overall traffic may fall by a lot more, and that will hurt ASO's ability to accelerate earnings growth. One thing to also be cautious about is the quality of traffic. Management did note that consumer engagement remains highly episodic, wherein they engage primarily during promotional events. If the traffic is proven to be non-organic (always driven by promotions), it would hurt the long-term growth outlook, as consumers may form the habit of waiting for promotions.

Conclusion

I upgrade to a buy rating for ASO because the underlying fundamentals now point to a healthier growth profile. The consumer mix shift is very positive, and the premium-brand “hook” strategy should continue to capture more traffic. With the unit-growth strategy also proving to work, I see a clearer path for earnings to compound into FY26 and beyond.

data.title: ArcBest: Stay Bullish That ARCB Can Hit Its EPS Guide
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to ArcBest Corp. (ARCB) as I believed it can achieve its 2028 adj. EPS guidance given the fundamental drivers. I maintain my buy rating because the drivers behind the 2028 EPS target continue to be intact. The November tonnage rebound, steady pricing, and the growing contribution from Managed Solutions reinforce my view that ARCB can grow earnings even in a flat cycle.

November’s Volume Inflection

The main debate around ARCB’s equity story is the weak volume trends. When management gave the Q4 2025 guide, it was mentioned that October was still weak, with tonnage per day down 1.2% y/y, and that signals to the market that Q4 will see a similar weak volume trend. But with the November data out, I think it effectively resolves that concern. In November, ARCB saw a sharp reversal, with tonnage per day up 3% y/y and daily shipments up 3% y/y.

This gave me more confidence that ARCB can indeed grow earnings as I expected. Back in October, my view was that ARCB could hit the target even in a flat cycle, with the assumption that volumes will not recover meaningfully. But with the 3% tonnage growth seen in November, which, if it holds into 2026, adds another positive attribute into the growth equation. Remember that the ARCB business model has a lot of fixed costs embedded, which means even a small volume gain can translate into decent earnings upside.

The context of this volume growth is even better if we put it against the fact that manufacturing PMI contracted for the eighth straight month in October. This tells me that ARCB is likely taking share, which seems right given management explicitly said shipments per day increased ~3% sequentially from October to November (note this is not in line with historical seasonality).

Pricing Discipline Remains Intact

The one data point that some readers may take wrongly in the 8-K filing is that in November, billed revenue per hundredweight fell 2% y/y. At face value, that looks like pricing softness. But in reality, it is really a problem of mix. Because of the excess capacity in the truckload market, some of the LTL freight volume has been shifted to truckload, and that shift itself causes the weight profile inside the LTL network to change too. When that happens, yield per hundredweight naturally moves because of the mathematical mechanism.

The better metric to track for pricing is what customers actually agreed to on renewals. You have to look at the Q3 earnings to derive this. In Q3, ARCB secured average increases of 4.5% on contract renewals and deferred pricing, up from 4% in Q2. That tells us that shippers are willing to pay more (you can see that as the clearing price). If the LTL market were deteriorating, you wouldn’t be seeing sequential acceleration in contract pricing. Management also reiterated during the call that the broader pricing environment remains rational.

As the truckload capacity supply situation eases and heavier freight returns to LTL (i.e., the weight for both sides normalizes), yield metrics should recover as well. It is also worth noting that revenue per hundredweight excluding fuel declined 5% sequentially in November at the same time weight per shipment increased 5%. The way to understand this is that heavier freight dilutes yield per pound but adds density and efficiency to the network. So, you shouldn’t look at pricing and ignore the other metrics. I believe ARCB is optimizing for profit and network flow, and the pricing trend doesn’t capture it.

Managed Solutions

One part of the story that I think still isn’t getting enough credit is the integrated model, especially the Managed Solutions business. The bears would typically just lump this segment into the low-margin brokerage bucket, but I believe they are wrong, and the recent numbers prove this.

Before I get there, to understand why the Managed Solutions segment matters, you have to ignore the “brokerage” label. Traditional truckload brokerage is transactional in nature (it’s the same as you hailing a taxi for a one-off trip). In this managed solution model, ARCB effectively becomes the customer's logistics team. Instead of bidding on individual loads, ARCB integrates its technology directly into the customer's daily operations to manage their entire freight flow. In terms of revenue quality, I’d say this is way higher than the core business since the switching cost is extremely high (costly and disruptive for the customer to switch providers). And since ARCB is managing the whole process, the volume becomes recurring and predictable.

As for the number proof, the data supports this view. The fact that Asset-Light total daily shipment saw 3% growth in Q3 despite a soft freight market shows how sticky these relationships are. Unlike the core transactional freight that tends to go away when competitors compete on rates, this volume is contractual and recurring.

Valuation

My valuation methodology hasn’t changed. I still think the best way to value ARCB is to look at the midterm guide and attach a multiple to it. My core view is that the targeted $13.5 EPS is possible, and in fact, I think there is a strong potential for EPS to even touch the high end of the guide, $15. If that is the case, I believe the current 14x NTM PE is for sure too low. Like I have compared before, even TFI International (TFII) trades at 18x with a similar earnings growth profile as ARCB (similar >20%). Again, my math is simple. If ARCB trades at the same multiple, the shares are worth $270.

Investment Risk

With PMI contracting for eight straight months, if it drags on any further, it would ultimately weigh on ARCB's overall growth, and that could delay the timeline for ARCB to hit its guided targets. There are also execution risks. For instance, the recent service issues drove a decline in Mastio rankings, and a failure to reverse this could hurt ARCB market share.

Conclusion

I stay bullish on ARCB because volumes are still stabilizing, pricing stays rational, and Managed Solutions is showing its worth. I believe the stock is still cheap if ARCB can hit its guided EPS, and the early signs from November suggest the freight cycle may be turning sooner than expected. If ARCB can deliver as expected, I expect multiples to go up, closing the gap vs. peers.

data.title: The Hartford: An Insurer I Remain Bullish On, As Its Business Insurance Niche Grows
data.description:
Today's Pick: A Major P&C Insurer With A Recent Earnings Beat

This past summer, I showed just how easy it can be to understand the insurance business, by writing about The Hartford Insurance Group (HIG), which has gone up around +6% since my buy rating in July.

In fact, this stock is up nearly 16% since an even earlier coverage of mine, in Oct. 2024, when I was also bullish.

This summer in particular, I recognized certain macroeconomic demand forecasts favoring the liability and employee benefits niche, but also pointed out the risk of catastrophic weather events when it comes to insuring property.

Besides those niches, according to its Seeking Alpha profile, this firm also offers workers' comp, car insurance, and marine, among others.

My updated coverage today takes into view some elements of its last earnings results reported in late October, when it beat estimates, and also adds some additional angles since my last article.

My Rating: Buy Reaffirmed

My thesis today argues to keep my prior buy rating on this stock.

The firm is one of my favorite insurers on the watchlist since I've began writing for this platform, and it has shown through several upside factors why it continues to be a bullish case, including being a steady dividend grower with strong credit ratings and low leverage risk, but also enough future upside expected in the share price, positive EPS estimates, and proven organic growth in key segments like business insurance.

Below is my worksheet which helps visualize how I got to this score and what factors drove it.

Long-Term Return vs S&P500: Neck to Neck With The Index

Today's analysis tries to be as future-looking as possible, trying to guess what the music may do in a year and beyond when it comes to this stock, and has longer-term investors in mind, however some historic-looking data also plays a role in the story.

In this first section, let's get a sense of the larger picture by comparing this stock's performance vs a major index like the S&P500, and how a few comparable peers did vs the index. This is to gauge if this stock has already proven itself in price performance, vs an investor having invested instead in an S&P500 index fund.

In this comparison, using momentum data, we see that Hartford had a 10 year price performance of +208%, putting it very close to the index which saw +241%, and also ahead of two key property/casualty insurers, Chubb (CB) and AIG (AIG).

Some readers have suggested the S&P500 is skewed heavily by major tech stocks, and that is understandable and I acknowledge such concerns, but nevertheless it remains a major market benchmark and this shows Hartford has a long track record of achieving competitive price performance vs the benchmark and some key peers, so for this reason I gave it a low bullish score in this category.

Sector Risk: The AI Opportunity Is There, But So is Ongoing Risk of Catastrophe Events

Let's talk about some risks and opportunities in the property/casualty insurance sector.

Unlike primarily life insurers, who insure mortality events but also may offer annuities/retirement solutions, we should think about P&C as a niche focusing on insuring homes, businesses, cars, and so on.

This also means it can see spikes in claims during major wildfires, weather events, and such, impacting profits that quarter, and from a future-view the risk is there is no 100% certainty this far in advance as to when and where a major weather event or fire may occur, however there are past statistics on certain regions more or less prone to those.

Last January, for instance, the Insurance Journal reported that:

Preliminary data show insurance companies have paid out more than $4 billion for losses from the biggest two of the Los Angeles-area wildfires that swept through the region and destroyed tens of thousands of homes earlier this month.

A potential opportunity in this sector, however, remains the potential of AI to change the productivity dynamic. For instance, the Boston Consulting Group wrote this past September:

The industry stands at the threshold of an AI-driven transformation that promises to reshape underwriting, claims, distribution, and customer engagement.

So, as a follower of AI trends, I see some of as an upside driver, if it can improve cost-effectiveness of the above processes without sacrificing service and the human experience as well, rather than replacing the human element. After all, my understanding is that insurance agents and reps have to be licensed in each US state before selling insurance, so there is still a barrier to entry as it is a highly regulated industry, also an upside as it limits the playing field. You can't just launch a startup in your garage and call yourself a licensed insurance agent, if you're not licensed.

Also, I want to point out signs of resilience in this industry, and resilience in the face of various market headwinds is one feature to look for in companies poised for future upside and stability.

Consider what industry group NAIC.org wrote earlier this year:

Despite heavy catastrophe losses, including the costliest wildfires on record, the U.S. Property & Casualty (P&C) industry recorded its best mid-year underwriting gain in nearly 20 years.

So, in an industry known for the need for prudent risk management, I think the opportunities for upside in this space are there, but of course some caution is needed due to the risk of catastrophe losses, hence I am in an upper neutral range with my score for this category.

Company Growth: Momentum in the Business Insurance Segment is Evident

In this section, let's talk about company-specific growth opportunities and risks, and we will use some points from Q3 earnings results to tell the story briefly.

First of all, the Hartford brand actually is more than just insurance, as according to its Q3 investor deck (pg. 3) it has a diversified revenue model that also includes an employee benefits line, and mutual funds (Hartford Funds).

I think this reduces future downside risk of over-reliance on a single product line, such as simply selling home insurance, and then bearing the brunt of wildfire or wind losses in a particular region.

On that note, we can see how this plays out in YoY earnings growth, as one business segment saw a slight YoY decline (employee benefits) but the other segments saw growth, with business insurance seeing +35% YoY core earnings growth, according to the same investor deck (pg 5):

When it comes to growth opportunity that drives future upside, in this sector I look for both organic growth (new insurance policies sold) as that can drive future premiums income, but also new business acquisitions, growth in premiums, and perhaps new moneys coming into the asset management segment like Hartford Funds.

The investor deck (pg 7) mentions within business insurance they saw growth in written premiums, "driven in part by strong new business growth."

That certainly is a sign of organic growth in one of their core business segments, business insurance.

In their mutual fund segment, the deck (pg 10) shows that although there are still net outflows to the firm's mutual funds/ETFs, there has been a considerable YoY improvement, with the outflows cut down to $25MM, so a major improvement trend starting, but more is needed to show positive net inflows over a longer period:

The mutual fund business is important as it is fees driven, so new net inflows of monies can contribute to future fees income, so the firm is not only reliant on insurance premiums or investment income.

When comparing against 4 peers in the P&C niche, Seeking Alpha data shows a positive upside factor, as Hartford is expected to have +6.9% fwd revenue growth, with only Allstate (ALL) beating it in this peer group:

So, with still some improvement to be made in the funds business, the otherwise notable momentum driver is the business insurance niche, so for the reasons above I gave this category a low bullish score.

Profit Margins: Competitive Positioning Among Large P&C Insurers, While AI Could Add Value

Here, let's consider some points about profit margins.

Since I love comparing to peers, the below table I pulled shows that Hartford is quite competitive to key peers in the P&C niche, and the two metrics I would highlight is the EBITDA and net income margins:

You'll notice something different than in all my recent coverage of REITs, and that is there is not such a wide gap between EBITDA and net margins like with many REITs, who I mentioned often use depreciation on property to bring down reported profits.

In the example above, the gap is not so pronounced, and this firm has exceeded 12% margins in both, but a key peer like Chubb is still well ahead, indicating to me that they may be somewhat more efficient at turning a profit that Hartford is.

Hartford is not a major outlier in this group, though, to warrant concern.

Still, as mentioned earlier, this business is prone to swings in catastrophe events impacting earnings. Its Q3 press release (pg 1) mentioned, for example:
